full_context: The correction of conductivity should use the following equation (C.1). This calculation is based on provisions in GB/T4585-2004 sections 16.1 and section 7. The formula is used to correct volume conductivity measured at various temperatures to its value at the standard temperature of 20°C, and is applicable for temperature conversion of volume conductivity in the cleaning solution on polluted insulator surfaces.

variable_definitions.name: Volume conductivity at 20°C
variable_definitions.meaning: Solution conductivity corrected to standard temperature

variable_definitions.name: Volume conductivity at temperature θ
variable_definitions.meaning: Solution conductivity measured at actual temperature

variable_definitions.name: Temperature coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction coefficient for conductivity change with temperature

variable_definitions.name: Solution temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual solution temperature at measurement

potential_questions: Measured θ=28°C, σ_θ=0.15 S/m, b=0.032/°C, find σ_20
task_description: 1. Measure solution temperature \(\theta\). 2. Measure solution conductivity \(\sigma_{\theta}\) at temperature \(\theta\). 3. Determine temperature coefficient \(b\). 4. Calculate temperature difference \((\theta-20)\). 5. Calculate \(b\times(\theta-20)\). 6. Calculate \([1-b\times(\theta-20)]\). 7. Compute \(\sigma_{20}=\sigma_{\theta}\times[1-b\times(\theta-20)]\). 8. Output \(\sigma_{20}\).

full_context: ESDD should be calculated according to Eq.(C.3) and Eq.(C.4) based on relevant GB/T4585-2004, section 16.2. The relationship between σ_20 and S_s (salinity kg/m³) is shown in Figure C.4. The formula is used to convert corrected conductivity to salinity, applicable for salinity calculation of pollutants on insulator surfaces.

variable_definitions.name: Salinity
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent concentration of dissolved salts in solution

variable_definitions.name: Volume conductivity at 20°C
variable_definitions.meaning: Solution conductivity corrected to standard temperature

potential_questions: Given σ_20=0.12 S/m, calculate S_s
task_description: 1. Obtain \(\sigma_{20}\) value. 2. Calculate \(5.7\times\sigma_{20}\). 3. Compute \((5.7\times\sigma_{20})^{1.03}\). 4. Output \(S_s\).

full_context: ESDD should be calculated according to Eq.(C.3) and Eq.(C.4). Formula (C.4) determines equivalent salt deposit density (ESDD) based on salinity and washing parameters and is suitable for quantitatively determining soluble deposit density on insulator surfaces.

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent salt deposit density
variable_definitions.meaning: Amount of equivalent salt deposited per unit area

variable_definitions.name: Salinity
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent concentration of dissolved salts in solution

variable_definitions.name: Volume of distilled water
variable_definitions.meaning: Volume of deionized water used for washing insulator

variable_definitions.name: Insulator surface area
variable_definitions.meaning: Effective area of insulator for pollution collection

potential_questions: Insulator with cleaning area A=1200 cm² washed with V=800 cm³ water, measured S_s=0.08 kg/m³, compute ESDD
task_description: 1. Determine salinity \(S_s\). 2. Measure distilled water volume \(V\) for cleaning. 3. Measure insulator effective surface area \(A\). 4. Compute \(S_s\times V\). 5. Compute \(\mathrm{ESDD}=(S_s\times V)/A\). 6. Perform unit conversion \((\mathrm{kg}/\mathrm{m}^3\cdot\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{cm}^2\rightarrow\mathrm{mg}/\mathrm{cm}^2)\). 7. Output \(\mathrm{ESDD}\).

formula: Average ESDD = (ESDD_t × A_t + ESDD_b × A_b) / A
full_context: When ESDD is measured separately for the top and bottom surfaces, the average ESDD is calculated as follows. This formula is used for calculating the overall average equivalent salt deposit density for insulators with double-sided structures, such as disc-type suspension insulators.

variable_definitions.name: Average equivalent salt deposit density
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent salt deposition density on the entire insulator surface

variable_definitions.name: Top surface ESDD
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent salt density on the top surface of the insulator

variable_definitions.name: Bottom surface ESDD
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent salt density on the bottom surface of the insulator

variable_definitions.name: Top surface area
variable_definitions.meaning: Effective area of the top surface of the insulator

variable_definitions.name: Bottom surface area
variable_definitions.meaning: Effective area of the bottom surface of the insulator

variable_definitions.name: Total surface area
variable_definitions.meaning: Total effective surface area of the insulator

potential_questions: Top surface: A_t=600 cm², ESDD_t=0.05 mg/cm²; Bottom surface: A_b=600 cm², ESDD_b=0.12 mg/cm², compute average ESDD
task_description: 1. Measure \(\mathrm{ESDD}_t\) and \(\mathrm{ESDD}_b\) for top and bottom surfaces. 2. Measure areas \(A_t\) and \(A_b\) for top and bottom surfaces. 3. Calculate \(\mathrm{ESDD}_t\times A_t\). 4. Calculate \(\mathrm{ESDD}_b\times A_b\). 5. Add numerators \((\mathrm{ESDD}_t\times A_t+\mathrm{ESDD}_b\times A_b)\). 6. Calculate total area \(A=A_t+A_b\). 7. Compute average \(\mathrm{ESDD}=(\mathrm{ESDD}_t\times A_t+\mathrm{ESDD}_b\times A_b)/A\). 8. Output result.

full_context: Use a funnel and pre-weighed, dried filter paper to filter water after measuring ESDD. Dry the filter paper containing the contaminant and weigh it again. NSDD is calculated by Eq.(C.6). This formula is for calculating insoluble deposit density, suitable for quantitative determination of insoluble pollutant deposition on insulator surfaces.

variable_definitions.name: Insoluble deposit density
variable_definitions.meaning: Amount of insoluble substance deposited per unit area

variable_definitions.name: Final dry weight of filter paper with contamination
variable_definitions.meaning: Dry weight of filter paper after collecting insoluble residue

variable_definitions.name: Initial dry weight of filter paper
variable_definitions.meaning: Dry weight of clean filter paper before filtration

variable_definitions.name: Insulator surface area
variable_definitions.meaning: Effective area of insulator for pollution collection

potential_questions: Filtered paper dry weight W_f=1.25g, initial weight W_i=1.00g, cleaning area A=1500 cm², calculate NSDD
task_description: 1. Weigh initial dry filter paper \(W_i\). 2. Filter cleaning liquid and dry the filter paper. 3. Weigh final dry filter paper \(W_f\) containing contaminants. 4. Compute paper weight increase \((W_f-W_i)\). 5. Measure effective insulator surface area \(A\). 6. Compute \(\mathrm{NSDD}=1000\times(W_f-W_i)/A\). 7. Output \(\mathrm{NSDD}\).

full_context: Correction for long-rod, post, and hollow insulators by average diameter D_a: use this formula when D_a ≥ 300 mm. The correction accounts for both the reduced performance of large-diameter insulators and lowered pollution accumulation.

variable_definitions.name: Diameter correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor for the effect of insulator diameter

variable_definitions.name: Average diameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Average diameter of insulator along creepage path

potential_questions: Insulator average diameter D_a=450 mm, find K_sd
task_description: 1. Calculate average diameter \(D_a\). 2. Compute \(0.0005\times D_a\). 3. Compute \(K_{sd}=0.0005\times D_a+0.85\). 4. Output \(K_{sd}\).

full_context: Creepage factor CF equals l/S, where l is the total creepage distance of the insulator and S is the arcing distance. For disc-type suspension insulators, CF is determined by a string of five or more insulators. Creepage factor is a comprehensive check on total creepage density.

variable_definitions.name: Creepage factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of creepage distance to arcing distance

variable_definitions.name: Total creepage distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Shortest creepage path between electrodes on insulator surface

variable_definitions.name: Arcing distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Shortest air gap between electrodes

potential_questions: Insulator string creepage distance l=2200 mm, arcing distance S=500 mm, find CF
task_description: 1. Measure total creepage distance \(l\). 2. Measure arcing distance \(S\). 3. Compute \(\mathrm{CF}=l/S\). 4. Output \(\mathrm{CF}\).

full_context: Where applicable, use the following equation to correct RUSCD determined by analysis in Chapter 9. All factors are multiplicative. The formula determines the final unified creepage distance, considering altitude and diameter, and is applicable to pollution design of AC porcelain and glass insulators.

variable_definitions.name: Unified creepage distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of creepage distance to maximum operating voltage

variable_definitions.name: Reference unified creepage distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Initial reference value for unified creepage distance

variable_definitions.name: Altitude correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor for high altitude influence

variable_definitions.name: Diameter correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor for large diameter influence

potential_questions: RUSCD=32 mm/kV, K_a=1.1, K_sd=0.92, find corrected USCD
task_description: 1. Determine \(\mathrm{RUSCD}\). 2. Obtain altitude correction factor \(K_a\). 3. Get diameter correction factor \(K_{sd}\). 4. Calculate \(\mathrm{USCD}=\mathrm{RUSCD}\times K_a\times K_{sd}\). 5. Output \(\mathrm{USCD}\).

full_context: Where applicable, correct RUSCD analyzed in Chapter 9 using: Corrected RUSCD = RUSCD × K_a × K_d. The formula is for final creepage distance calculation for composite insulators considering altitude and diameter.

variable_definitions.name: Corrected reference creepage distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Creepage distance after altitude and diameter correction

variable_definitions.name: Reference unified creepage distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Initial value of reference creepage distance

variable_definitions.name: Altitude correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor for altitude

variable_definitions.name: Diameter correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor for large diameter

potential_questions: RUSCD=28 mm/kV, K_a=1.05, K_sd=0.95, find corrected RUSCD
task_description: 1. Get \(\mathrm{RUSCD}\). 2. Get altitude correction factor \(K_a\). 3. Get diameter correction factor \(K_{sd}\). 4. Calculate corrected \(\mathrm{RUSCD}=\mathrm{RUSCD}\times K_a\times K_{sd}\). 5. Output result.

formula: Voltage qualification rate = (1 - (voltage exceedance time / total voltage monitoring time)) × 100%
full_context: Reactive power and voltage assessment points for PV power stations are at grid connection. Voltage and reactive monitoring should use instruments with continuous monitoring and statistics capability according to DL/T 448. The formula is: voltage qualification rate = (1 - [voltage exceedance time / total voltage monitoring time]) × 100%. This formula evaluates the proportion of time voltage is within allowable deviation at the grid point—an important metric for voltage stability.

variable_definitions.name: Voltage qualification rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Percentage of time actual voltage is within permissible deviation during monitoring

variable_definitions.name: Voltage exceedance time
variable_definitions.meaning: Accumulated time voltage exceeds permissible deviation (see DL/T 448 for definition; duration voltage exceeds standard limit during monitoring)

variable_definitions.name: Total voltage monitoring time
variable_definitions.meaning: Total operating time for monitoring statistics (see DL/T 448 for definition; total monitoring duration)

potential_questions: A PV station grid point voltage exceedance time is 1800s, total voltage monitoring time 86400s (one day), calculate the qualification rate
task_description: 1. Measure or obtain voltage exceedance time \(t_{\text{exceed}}\) (s). 2. Measure or obtain total voltage monitoring time \(t_{\text{total}}\) (s). 3. Compute ratio \(t_{\text{exceed}}/t_{\text{total}}\). 4. Compute \(1- t_{\text{exceed}}/t_{\text{total}}\). 5. Multiply by 100 to get percentage. 6. Output voltage qualification rate.

full_context: Required pressure relief area for buildings should be calculated as: A = 10CV^{2/3}, but if the length-to-width ratio of the building exceeds 3, the building should be divided into segments with length-to-width ratio not exceeding 3, and shared cross-sections among segments should not be considered for pressure relief area. Here, A is the pressure relief area (m²); V is the volume of the building (m³); C is the pressure relief coefficient (see table 3.6.4, m²/m³). The formula is used for calculating required pressure relief area to prevent explosion pressure from damaging the structure.

variable_definitions.name: Pressure relief area
variable_definitions.meaning: Opening area for pressure relief against explosions

variable_definitions.name: Building volume
variable_definitions.meaning: Total volume of the explosion-prone space

variable_definitions.name: Pressure relief ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Required pressure relief area per unit volume

potential_questions: Given building with length 30m, width 20m, height 8m, pressure relief ratio C=0.11, calculate the pressure relief area
task_description: 1. Calculate building volume \(V=\text{length}\times\text{width}\times\text{height}\). 2. Determine pressure relief ratio \(C\) (from table 3.6.4). 3. Calculate \(V^{2/3}\). 4. Compute \(A=10\times C\times V^{2/3}\). 5. Output pressure relief area \(A\).

full_context: Length-to-width ratio in building plan geometry is determined as: product of maximum dimension L and cross-section perimeter P divided by 4 times the building cross-sectional area A. When the ratio exceeds 3, segmental pressure relief area calculation is required.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum dimension
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum span in building plan

variable_definitions.name: Cross-sectional perimeter
variable_definitions.meaning: Perimeter of building cross-section outline

variable_definitions.name: Cross-sectional area
variable_definitions.meaning: Cross-sectional area of building

potential_questions: Rectangular building with length 40m, cross-section 12m × 8m, calculate length-to-width ratio
task_description: 1. Determine maximum building dimension \(L\). 2. Calculate cross-section perimeter \(P\). 3. Calculate cross-sectional area \(A\). 4. Compute length-to-width ratio \(=(L\times P)/(4A)\). 5. Decide if it's \(>3\).

full_context: Total volume of fixed-volume combustible gas tank is calculated as the product of tank geometric volume V_{geom} (m³) and design storage pressure P (absolute, in 10⁵Pa).

variable_definitions.name: Total volume
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent volume of tank

variable_definitions.name: Geometric volume
variable_definitions.meaning: Physical volume of tank

variable_definitions.name: Design storage pressure
variable_definitions.meaning: Design pressure for tank operation

potential_questions: Tank geometric volume 50m³, design pressure 1.2MPa, calculate total volume
task_description: 1. Get geometric volume \(V_{\mathrm{geom}}\). 2. Get design pressure \(P\). 3. Compute \(V_{\mathrm{total}}=V_{\mathrm{geom}}\times P\). 4. Output total volume \(V_{\mathrm{total}}\).

full_context: The canopy of pedestrian street should have a lower eave height not less than 6.0m, natural smoke ventilation openings should be installed, and their effective area should not be less than 37% of the pedestrian street ground area.

variable_definitions.name: Opening area
variable_definitions.meaning: Effective area of smoke exhaust vent

variable_definitions.name: Ground area
variable_definitions.meaning: Total pedestrian street ground area

potential_questions: Pedestrian street ground area 1500m², calculate minimum smoke exhaust opening area
task_description: 1. Measure pedestrian street ground area \(A_{\mathrm{ground}}\). 2. Compute minimum opening area \(A_{\mathrm{open}}=0.37\times A_{\mathrm{ground}}\). 3. Design layout for smoke exhaust openings.

full_context: Sunken plaza and similar open outdoor spaces should prevent fire spread to adjacent areas and ensure safe evacuation, with minimum net area not less than 169m².

variable_definitions.name: Minimum net area
variable_definitions.meaning: Safe area for fire spread prevention

potential_questions: Check if a sunken plaza of 13m × 13m meets area requirement
task_description: 1. Measure area of sunken plaza \(A\). 2. Check if \(A\ge 169\,\mathrm{m}^2\). 3. Adjust design until specification is met.

full_context: For a sloped roof, the building height is the average of the elevation from outdoor design ground to the eave and the ridge.

variable_definitions.name: Building height
variable_definitions.meaning: Reference height for sloped roof buildings

variable_definitions.name: Eave height
variable_definitions.meaning: Height at the lowest roof point

variable_definitions.name: Ridge height
variable_definitions.meaning: Height at the highest roof point

potential_questions: Eave height 24m, ridge height 30m, calculate building height
task_description: 1. Measure eave height \(H_1\). 2. Measure ridge height \(H_2\). 3. Calculate average height \(H=(H_1+H_2)/2\). 4. Determine building classification.

full_context: Fire separation distance between buildings should be calculated as the minimum horizontal distance between adjacent exterior walls. If there are protruding combustible or flame-retardant elements on exterior walls, measure from the outermost edge of the protrusion.

variable_definitions.name: Fire separation distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum distance for fire spread prevention

variable_definitions.name: Wall-to-wall distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Horizontal distance between points on adjacent exterior walls

potential_questions: Building A exterior wall protrudes 3m, primary structure of building B is 15m away; calculate actual fire separation distance
task_description: 1. Measure all horizontal wall-to-wall distances \(d_i\). 2. Take the minimum value as \(D=\min(d_1,d_2,\ldots)\). 3. Check if it meets code requirements.

full_context: Water use index for power plant design should meet requirements in table 18.1.8: W = Q / P, where W is water use index, Q is total plant water consumption, P is installed capacity. This formula is for the evaluation of water use efficiency under different cooling modes, and is a key indicator of water resource utilization.

variable_definitions.name: Water use index
variable_definitions.meaning: Water use per unit installed capacity

variable_definitions.name: Total plant water consumption
variable_definitions.meaning: Total water used in power plant operations

variable_definitions.name: Installed capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Sum of rated capacity for all generators in the plant

potential_questions: Installed capacity 300MW, total fresh water circulation 0.45m³/s, compute water use index
task_description: 1. Determine type of cooling system. 2. Obtain \(Q\) (total water consumption). 3. Obtain \(P\) (installed capacity, convert to GW if necessary). 4. Calculate \(W=Q/P\). 5. Compare with table 18.1.8 for compliance.

full_context: Boiler feedwater treatment system output is calculated as: V = k × Q × t, where V is system output, k is margin coefficient, Q is rated evaporation of the boiler, t is design run time. This formula determines the design capacity of the feedwater treatment system, ensuring normal and emergency operation.

variable_definitions.name: System output
variable_definitions.meaning: Design treatment capacity of feedwater system

variable_definitions.name: Margin coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Safety factor for redundancy and expansion

variable_definitions.name: Boiler rated evaporation
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated steam output of the boiler

variable_definitions.name: Design run time
variable_definitions.meaning: Continuous run time for system benchmark

potential_questions: Boiler rated evaporation Q=410t/h, margin coefficient k=1.2, design run time t=24h, compute system output
task_description: 1. Obtain rated evaporation \(Q\). 2. Obtain margin coefficient \(k\). 3. Get design run time \(t\). 4. Calculate \(V=k\times Q\times t\). 5. Confirm it meets maximum requirement.

full_context: Head of hydraulic ash disposal pump is calculated as: G = (Q × η) / H, G is pump head, Q is ash mass, η is conveying efficiency, H is geometric head. This formula is for calculating pump head for hydraulic ash handling systems.

variable_definitions.name: Ash pump head
variable_definitions.meaning: Pump head required to overcome system resistance

variable_definitions.name: Ash amount
variable_definitions.meaning: Ash mass delivered per unit time

variable_definitions.name: Conveying efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Efficiency coefficient of the conveying system

variable_definitions.name: Geometric head
variable_definitions.meaning: Elevation difference between discharge and intake

potential_questions: Ash amount Q=50t/h, η=0.85, geometric head H=30m, find required pump head
task_description: 1. Measure ash amount \(Q\). 2. Get conveying efficiency \(\eta\). 3. Measure geometric head \(H\). 4. Compute \(G=(Q\times\eta)/H\). 5. Select appropriate pump for the required head.

full_context: Condensate pump head is calculated as: H_p = ΔP + ΔZ + v²/(2g), where ΔP is system resistance, ΔZ is geometric head difference, v is velocity, g is gravitational constant. The formula helps determine pump head for reliable operation in the condensate system.

variable_definitions.name: Condensate pump head
variable_definitions.meaning: Total head required to overcome all system resistance

variable_definitions.name: System resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Pressure losses in pipes and equipment

variable_definitions.name: Geometric height difference
variable_definitions.meaning: Water level difference between condenser and deaerator

variable_definitions.name: Fluid velocity
variable_definitions.meaning: Average flow velocity in the pipe

variable_definitions.name: Gravitational acceleration
variable_definitions.meaning: Earth's acceleration due to gravity

potential_questions: System resistance ΔP=15m, elevation difference ΔZ=20m, velocity v=2m/s, calculate required pump head
task_description: 1. Calculate system resistance \(\Delta P\). 2. Measure geometric height difference \(\Delta Z\). 3. Determine flow velocity \(v\). 4. Use \(g=9.81\,\mathrm{m/s^2}\). 5. Calculate \(H_p=\Delta P+\Delta Z+\frac{v^2}{2g}\).

full_context: Reactive compensation capacity should preferably be determined by the reactive power curve or by the formula: Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2). Q_c is the compensation capacity (kvar); P is the calculated active power (kW); \tan\phi_1 is the tangent of the natural power factor before compensation; \tan\phi_2 is the tangent of the power factor after compensation, with \cos\phi_2 no less than 0.9. The formula helps determine the required compensation device for a power system.

variable_definitions.name: Reactive compensation capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Required reactive power for compensation

variable_definitions.name: Active power
variable_definitions.meaning: Calculated active power

variable_definitions.name: Tangent of initial power factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Tangent of natural power factor before compensation

variable_definitions.name: Tangent of target power factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Tangent corresponding to target \cos\phi_2 ≥ 0.9

potential_questions: Given P=100kW, tanφ₁=0.75, target cosφ₂=0.95, calculate Q_c
task_description: 1. Determine active power \(P\). 2. Get or calculate tangent of initial power factor \(\tan\phi_1\). 3. Compute tangent of target power factor \(\tan\phi_2\) according to target \(\cos\phi_2\). 4. Compute \(Q_c=P\times(\tan\phi_1-\tan\phi_2)\). 5. Output \(Q_c\).

full_context: Basic reactive compensation capacity must satisfy: Q_{Cmin} ≤ P_{min} tanφ_{1min}. Q_{Cmin} is the minimum compensation (kvar); P_{min} is minimum active power (kW); tanφ_{1min} is tangent of power factor at minimum load before compensation. The formula helps in minimum compensation sizing for distribution system design.

variable_definitions.name: Minimum compensation capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum reactive power required under lowest load

variable_definitions.name: Minimum active power
variable_definitions.meaning: Active power at minimum load

variable_definitions.name: Tangent of minimum load power factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Tangent of power factor at minimum load before compensation

potential_questions: P_min=50kW, tanφ_{1min}=0.8, calculate maximum Q_{Cmin}
task_description: 1. Determine minimum load active power \(P_{\min}\). 2. Obtain tangent of minimum load power factor \(\tan\phi_{1\min}\). 3. Calculate \(Q_{C\min}\le P_{\min}\times\tan\phi_{1\min}\). 4. Output maximum allowed \(Q_{C\min}\).

full_context: Selection of protective conductor cross-sectional area must satisfy: S = I / k × √t. S is cross-sectional area (mm²); I is the expected fault or short-circuit current RMS (A); t is the protection device operation time for fault shut-off (s); k is the material coefficient (see Appendix A). This formula ensures the conductor withstands thermal effects during fault; applicable for low voltage systems.

variable_definitions.name: Protective conductor cross-sectional area
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum cross-sectional area for protective conductor

variable_definitions.name: Expected fault current
variable_definitions.meaning: Expected RMS value of fault current

variable_definitions.name: Operation time
variable_definitions.meaning: Protection device fault shut-off time

variable_definitions.name: Material coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Computation coefficient related to conductor material (see Appendix A)

potential_questions: I=10kA, t=0.2s, k=143 (Cu), compute minimum S
task_description: 1. Determine expected fault current \(I\). 2. Obtain protection device operation time \(t\). 3. Get \(k\) value for conductor material. 4. Compute \(S=I\times\sqrt{t}/k\). 5. Output \(S\) (mm²).

full_context: Material coefficient determined by the following formula: k = sqrt(Q_c(β+20)/ρ_20 × ln(1+ (θ_f-θ_i)/(β+θ_i))). Where k is the coefficient; Q_c is material specific heat at 20°C [J/(°C·mm³)]; β is reciprocal of resistivity temperature coefficient at 0°C (°C); ρ_20 is resistivity at 20°C (Ω·mm); θ_i is initial temperature (°C); θ_f is final temperature (°C). Used for calculating thermal stability coefficient k in cross-section design for low-voltage protective conductors.

variable_definitions.name: Thermal stability coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Material thermal stability computation coefficient

variable_definitions.name: Specific heat per volume
variable_definitions.meaning: Material volume specific heat at 20°C

variable_definitions.name: Reciprocal of resistivity temp coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Reciprocal of resistivity temp coefficient at 0°C

variable_definitions.name: Resistivity at 20°C
variable_definitions.meaning: Material electrical resistivity at 20°C

variable_definitions.name: Initial temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Initial conductor temperature

variable_definitions.name: Final temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Final conductor temperature

task_description: 1. Obtain \(Q_c\), \(\beta\), \(\rho_{20}\) for material. 2. Obtain initial temperature \(\theta_i\) and final temperature \(\theta_f\). 3. Calculate \((\theta_f-\theta_i)/(\beta+\theta_i)\). 4. Compute \(\ln\left(1+\frac{\theta_f-\theta_i}{\beta+\theta_i}\right)\). 5. Calculate numerator \(Q_c\times(\beta+20)\). 6. Calculate denominator \(\rho_{20}\). 7. Compute \([Q_c(\beta+20)/\rho_{20}]\times\ln\left(1+\frac{\theta_f-\theta_i}{\beta+\theta_i}\right)\). 8. Take square root for \(k\). 9. Output \(k\).

full_context: Overload protection device operation characteristics must meet: I_B ≤ I_n ≤ I_Z and I_2 ≤ 1.45 I_Z. I_B is circuit calculated current; I_n is rated current of fuse, breaker, or setting; I_Z is permitted continuous current for conductor; I_2 is minimum current to ensure device operation. Formula validates matching of overload protection device and conductor to guarantee prompt fault interruption in low-voltage distribution lines.

variable_definitions.name: Calculated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Circuit working current for calculation

variable_definitions.name: Protection device rated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated current of fuse or breaker

variable_definitions.name: Permitted conductor current
variable_definitions.meaning: Permitted long-term current for conductor

variable_definitions.name: Protection action current
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum current to ensure device operates reliably

potential_questions: Given I_B=85A, I_Z=100A, check if I_n=90A meets requirements
task_description: 1. Obtain calculated circuit current \(I_B\). 2. Obtain permitted conductor current \(I_Z\). 3. Choose rated current for protection device \(I_n\). 4. Verify \(I_B\le I_n\le I_Z\). 5. Obtain protection action current \(I_2\) (breaker setting). 6. Verify \(I_2\le 1.45\times I_Z\). 7. Output verification result.

full_context: TT system indirect contact protection device operation: R_A I_a ≤ 50. R_A is the sum of ground resistance of exposed conductive part and protective conductor resistance (Ω); I_a is operating current to ensure device auto shutdown in specified time (A). Formula ensures contact voltage does not exceed 50V in TT systems and is used for shock protection design.

variable_definitions.name: Total grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Sum of exposed conductive part ground resistance and protective conductor resistance

variable_definitions.name: Protection action current
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum operating current to ensure protection device operates in time

potential_questions: Given R_A=10Ω, find minimum I_a
task_description: 1. Measure ground resistance for exposed conductive parts. 2. Calculate protective conductor resistance. 3. Add for \(R_A\). 4. Determine operating current \(I_a\) for protection device. 5. Verify \(R_A\times I_a\le 50\). 6. If not met, adjust device or resistance as required.
